data.title: Iovance's Q4: Execution Risks Mount As Infusion Growth Slows (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Iovance Biotherapeutics, Inc.’s (NASDAQ:IOVA) valuation has been slashed in half since my last evaluation in August.

Recall that Iovance’s Amtagvi, the first TIL therapy product to achieve FDA approval in February 2024, is marketed for the treatment of advanced melanoma.

My last look followed Amtagvi’s first full quarter on the market. Amtagvi delivered $12.8 million in revenue. I upgraded IOVA from “hold” to “buy” due to the “improved outlook for Amtagvi.” However, I also noted “significant risks,” including competition and the inherent challenges associated with TIL therapy like “cost, intensity, and complexity.”

Subsequently, although I was bullish on IOVA, I thought it was most appropriate in a barbell portfolio with limited allocation.

The paragraphs that follow catch up to present day to provide an updated take on IOVA.

Q3 Performance

In Q3, Amtagvi brought in $42.1 million in product revenue. Iovance noted that:

“a total of 146 patients have been infused with Amtagvi since the first commercial infusion in April 2024, including 25 patients infused in the second quarter, 82 patients infused in the third quarter, and 39 patients infused since the start of the fourth quarter.”

Iovance also markets Proleukin, an IL-2 therapy, which is “used in the Amtagvi treatment regimen.” Proleukin revenues were $16.5 million. Iovance reiterated their revenue projections of “$160 to $165 million” and “$450 to $475 million” for FY24 and FY25, respectively. The company also noted 56 authorized treatment centers (ATCs) and a manufacturing turnaround time of “approximately 34 days from inbound to return shipment to ATCs.” Iovance ended Q3 with $397.488 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments.

Q4 Performance

Yesterday, Iovance reported Q4 revenue of $73.7 million, slightly below analyst estimates. Amtagvi and Proleukin revenues were $48.7 million and $25 million, respectively. The company met its FY24 revenue guidance. The cost of sales was $45.543 million. R&D and SG&A expenses totaled $72.224 million and $42.503 million, respectively. Iovance's net loss for Q4 was $78.559 million.

Interestingly, Iovance did not report infusion numbers. During the earnings call, Interim CEO and President, Frederick Vogt, responded defensively when an analyst inquired about “the number of patients infused thus far in the quarter.”

We're not going to be providing the infusions right now. We're not sure how useful that metric was to long investors. And as some of you know, it was prone to overinterpretation.

Minutes later, after being pressed about Q4 infusion numbers, Mr. Vogt provided some color: “95 revenue infusions.”

Mr. Vogt then compared the Q/Q variability to when CAR-T products first came out. So, we will see if the slowdown is sustained, as this could signal access or adoption bottlenecks.

Active ATCs totaled 70 across 32 states, with only 13% of centers having treated 10 patients. So, there is plenty of room for expansion. Iovance hopes to eventually treat >10,000 patients annually.

Turnaround time remained “approximately 34 days,” with “efforts underway to shorten the turnaround time in 2025.” Reducing the turnaround time is critical, as patients must remain clinically stable while awaiting treatment.

Iovance’s cash burn remains significant, with a guidance of “under $300 million” in cash burn for 2025. As of February 26, the company's cash, cash equivalents, investments, and restricted cash were “approximately $422 million.” This is expected to provide a cash runway “into the second half of 2026.”

Looking ahead, Iovance reaffirmed their FY25 revenue guidance of “$450 to $475 million.”

Adoption & Pricing Strategy

Amtagvi remains a preferred option in PD-1-refractory melanoma. Past salvage therapies like chemotherapy and CTLA-4 immunotherapy had much lower response rates than Amtagvi's 31.5% ORR. While oncologists have been receptive to TIL therapy, they also recognize the need for improvements (e.g., cost, safety, convenience).

Iovance has faced no major issues with payers despite Amtagvi’s hefty $515,000 price tag. This is likely due to Amtagvi’s demonstrated efficacy in an otherwise untreatable population. As a result, Iovance intends to increase the price of Amtagvi this year, aiming to deliver >70% margins over time.

Pipeline & Future Growth

In the clinic, Amtagvi’s confirmatory Phase 3 trial (TILVANCE-301) remains underway. This trial is also testing Amtagvi in combination with Keytruda in frontline melanoma, which could both fulfill the confirmation requirement and expand the label. Its primary completion is estimated for March 2028.

For their first-generation TIL therapy, Iovance is also seeking indications in other cancers like NSCL and endometrial, aiming for potential approval in the former by 2027.

Beyond Amtagvi, Iovance is developing next-generation TIL therapies:

  • IOV-4001, which uses gene-editing to knock out the PD-1 gene in the TIL cells (Phase 1 for melanoma and NSCLC).
  • IOV-3001, a modified IL-2 analog intended to negate the need for high-dose IL-2, which could improve safety and convenience.
  • A newer-generation TIL therapy product that allows for 16-day manufacturing in Phase 2 development.

Rising Competition: Obsidian & Beyond

Iovance enjoys a first-mover advantage, as Amtagvi is the only approved cell therapy for solid tumors on the market. However, competition looms.

For instance, Obsidian Therapeutics and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) are co-advancing an enhanced TIL product (OBX-115) that engineers TIL cells to produce IL-15, potentially bypassing the need for high-dose IL-2. Obsidian is recruiting patients with advanced solid tumors in a Phase 1/2 trial (NCT06060613).

Others are also working on making TILs more potent, cheaper, and easier to deliver. So, while Iovance has secured a niche in advanced melanoma, it is imperative for them to innovate.

Investor Sentiment: A Popular Short-Seller Target

The market is very cautious and, perhaps, rightly so.

Iovance’s TIL therapy is complex and costly to scale. Iovance’s expenses are hefty, and it remains in a loss-making phase with dwindling cash reserves. This has made IOVA a popular target for shorts, with its short interest nearing 21%, which is unusually high. In the past year, IOVA is down well over 75%.

IOVA Stock: Balanced Take & Investment Recommendation

Looking back at my rating upgrade following Amtagvi's first full quarter on the market, I may have been too quick to change my tone. Recent developments support a more cautious stance (rating downgrade to “hold”).

I can understand both bear and bull points at this time. So, I am neutral on IOVA. The stock could feasibly continue on its downward spiral or explode upwards at a moment's notice. Given the long-awaited medical advancement of TIL therapy that is currently “monopolized” by Iovance, I can still see IOVA's utility in a barbell portfolio. However, investors should be aware of the many risks discussed above and allocate no more than what they can afford to lose.

data.title: Nvidia's Solid Earnings, Jobs Expectations And Fighting Inflation
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our director of news at Seeking Alpha, welcome back to Wall Street Breakfast. Great to have you as always.

Brian Stewart: Great to be back.

RS: Talk to us about where we sit, what you're most focused on, what you think the market is most focused on.

Brian Stewart: We're professionally bound to talk about NVIDIA (NVDA) at the start. The earnings just came out. The stock was down about 4% after earnings. The earnings themselves were were very solid, beating expectations, stronger than expected guidance, but the stock is still down a bit.

To be fair, the stock was up a bit beforehand. It was up about 4% the day before its earnings report. So this is just giving back that pre earnings enthusiasm.

I think the general feeling is steady as she goes. NVIDIA's about 25% off of its highs. So the idea is we're not in bubble territory yet for the AI, but there are signs of slowing growth.

NVIDIA data center revenues, that's the area that's very AI focused. That showed 93% growth this last quarter, which obviously is very strong, but it's down from 112% growth in the previous quarter.

And that number was in the 400% range a year ago. So you're seeing the peak of AI growth past for NVIDIA. And so the question is where is that that growth number going to settle in?

In terms of general stock performance is that chips are down generally. And this isn't even just in response to NVIDIA earnings. This is over the past week.

So we have (AMD) down 9%, (ARM) is down 10%. We've flagged ARM as a good AI bellwether just because it was it was part of the growth plan that people have put in place.

And then Broadcom (AVGO) and Micron (MU) are also down. So I don't think it's a NVIDIA specific story. I think this is a market referendum on the AI situation in general.

So I think that it's TBD about where things are going. I think for the most part, people are happy to tread water at this point and and wait for more data.

RS: And what else would you take in terms of anything to extrapolate broader speaking for how investors see upcoming earnings for any similar players?

BS: So next week we have Broadcom coming out on Thursday. We have Marvell (MRVL) as well. So there is gonna be some data points in the chip area on the horizon. Those are two key areas to look for for next week.

If we broaden the lens a little bit and look for technology in general and not just chips, we have a bunch of cybersecurity companies coming out next week. I think the biggest of those is gonna be CrowdStrike (CRWD).

CrowdStrike was down sharply last year after its highly publicized outage in July, but has crawled back above those levels recently. However, it reached a fifty two week high early response, but it's seen a dip lately.

So I think those earnings are gonna be key for CrowdStrike, specifically about whether or not it's moved past that outage and is back to regular business as usual.

And then I think just looking at cybersecurity in general, I think that's an interesting place to look for technology in general, but also as we look at the new Trump administration spending priorities, whether or not companies with a strong public balance sheet, as you will, strong governmental revenues, whether those companies are worried about cuts coming up.

RS: Any other names in the tech sector that you'd care to highlight at this point?

BS: I think it's a good time to peek in on Tesla (TSLA). It's down 19% in the past week. This is part of a general slide. It's down 30% year to date and 42% from its fifty two week highs late last year.

There's a lot of worry about the Elon Musk distraction. He's busy with DOGE and not only does that take him away from running Tesla, but it's also becoming not a great look in general. There's talk of of boycotting Tesla. It's just becoming a very high profile and not particularly well liked presence at this point.

There's also been data this week from a European industry group, the European Automobile Manufacturers Associations that Tesla sales in Europe were down 45% in January. That could be a sign of sort of dissatisfaction with Musk specifically.

Overall, EV sales were up in Europe in the same dataset. So the fact that Tesla was down so sharply might be something very Tesla related. The fall has brought Tesla below $1 trillion in market cap for the first time since November.

So you can see the upside and downside of his close association with the Trump administration, you saw Tesla rally sharply after the election, but now that there's much more controversy around his activities in relation to the government, you're starting to see that have an effect on Tesla.

RS: You certainly are. Much more discussion in the marketplace around how Elon Musk's companies are faring because Elon Musk is in charge.

Speaking of public sentiment and people in charge, there's some discussion around insurance company this week due to proposed Medicaid cuts. What would you highlight in that regard?

BS: Yeah. UnitedHealth (UNH) was down 9% this week. As you said, the proposed Medicaid cuts are starting to raise red flags. That's a big part of their business.

There's also a DOJ probe of its Medicare billing practices. So the stock in general is down 14%, since early February. So this is starting to seep into the market, the idea that there's less government support in general.

Another example is Palantir (PLTR). There's concerns budget cuts have taken some of the air out of that stock. It was up 24% after its earnings report in early February, but has recently had a five session losing streak.

And that was a pretty dramatic drop. Each of those five sessions, it was down 3%, and then two of them were a 10% or more drop.

So you're starting to see, even if it's individual companies or sectors, starting to see the ripple effect of those budget cuts that are being put into place, or at least the proposed budget cuts.

And then in general, if we take it back to a more macro level and look at the economic data, people are starting to get worried about the economy in general. We saw initial jobless claims jump to a multi month high in the most recent data. And so you're starting to see worries about layoffs.

This is critical ahead of next week's jobs data, which is a little backward looking, but still, I think people are gonna be looking at that really closely to see if the the cut in public public jobs is is starting to have a a broader economic impact.

RS: What's the sentiment or expectations heading into that?

BS: I think they're starting to to lower a little bit. The job market has been one of the bright spots going as we've been fighting inflation, the fact that the job market has held up as well as it has has given the Fed permission to take the inflation fight more seriously than it could otherwise if it also had to worry about the economy.

But if you have an economy that's starting to show some cracks, it puts the Fed in a real bind because if it cuts rates to protect the economy, then that could unleash the inflation goblin.

But if you fight inflation, you might undermine any attempts to jump start the economy. So you end up in a situation where the Fed is stuck between a rock and a hard place.

Another troublesome data point that affected the markets this week is the conference board's consumer confidence index dropped to 98.3 in February. This was down from January. That's the biggest monthly fall since August of 2021. Anytime you're saying biggest whatever since the COVID, shutdowns, you're probably in sort of frightening territory.

So one of the sub indexes in there is the expectation index dropped 9.3 points to 72.9. That's below the 80 mark. That's usually seen as a recession red flag.

So you see consumers in general are just starting to look around and see high inflation, see an economy that doesn't seem to be be as solid as it once was, and they're starting to get worried.

One thing that I would say is next week, there's a lot of retailers on the earnings docket. So in terms of getting a vibe about what consumers are thinking, I think those earnings reports are going to be important. One of the key ones is Costco (COST). They're coming out Thursday.

The company is recently off a fifty two week high. It's gotten a lot of run-in the fact that it's a low price competitor. And so it it brings in a lot of people who are worried about inflation and looking to find deals.

Those are your Costco customers. But with the increases come valuation concerns, so there's worries that whether Costco can keep up the momentum that it's had.

And then elsewhere, you have a lot of department stores like Macy's (M) and Nordstrom's (JWN) and then other clothing retailers like like Gap (GAP) are reporting.

So like I said to get an idea of the spending habits of the general consumer, I think taking those reports in aggregate would be a good data source.

The biggest preliminary to the wave of reports that's coming that we had was the Walmart (WMT) earnings, which sent the stock down a little bit. There was just worries that it couldn't keep up the momentum it had.

Walmart's in a special case because it has the benefit of size plus the low price business plan works well in an inflationary environment.

So companies that have sort of a higher price level like Nordstrom's, it'll be interesting to see whether or not they can still draw people in or whether people are looking around for cheaper deals.

And another data point to be drawn from that, and this will be more from the conference calls and other commentary from management, is just whether or not there's concern about tariffs and whether or not that's going to interrupt supply chains for some of these big retailers.

RS: Have we seen any tariff effects yet that are concrete examples of what happens when tariffs are enacted? Do we have any data yet to go on with that?

BS: I haven't seen any, the earnings reports that are coming out are obviously a little backward looking. You're looking at least six weeks backwards as the company put together their earnings reports when the quarters ends.

But there are there are some commentaries coming out. For instance, Budweiser (BUD) just released its earnings.

It's up about 11% in the past week. It jumped 7% on its earnings report. It said that it sees only limited impact from tariffs. So that's a global company saying that it probably won't see a big hit from tariffs.

In the sales in general, revenue was up 3%, but volumes were down 2%. So you have a situation where a company is making up for lower volumes with with higher prices and then getting help from cost management from keeping costs low.

So in that particular case, it's basically a company that's managing its business well in in a difficult environment. So you're bound to find other companies that aren't managing as well in a difficult environment.

And so it becomes just a much, much more sensitive process trying to kind of tiptoe through the landline.

One thing to note about tariffs in general is the the companies don't pay them per se. Some might make the decision just to eat as much of the tariffs as they can, but other ones are gonna just pass it on to customers.

So you might see a a rush of higher prices and this isn't tariff related, but you've seen companies put in, extra pricing for the eggs, as egg prices have gone up.

So you do see companies find ways to pass these costs onto consumers. So whether or not a company can do that is going to be based on their individual business. So it's really going to be kind of company specific.

And so if you look at Budweiser in general, it seems like so far they've been pretty successful in that. And you can also see that filtering through to the consumer level in the conference board consumer confidence index report.

The inflation expectations, consumers inflation expectations jumped to 6% from just over 5% in the previous month. Just as a reference, the Fed is looking to keep inflation about 2%.

So you have consumers seeing predicting a 3x to the Fed's benchmark. So the consumers are pretty ready for higher inflation, so that might give some companies a green light to go ahead and raise prices.

On an ending note, I'd just underline again the jobs data that's coming out. I think that's gonna be critical, especially as you're looking for Fed policy. Like I said, the Fed is starting to find itself in a trap, and so that'll be the key to whether or not it has some some inflation fighting room left to go.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hidden Value: Tax-Loss Harvesting In Up Markets
data.description:
By Jacob Greene

Selling for tax purposes is not just viable during periods of market weakness.

A common misconception about tax-loss harvesting strategies is that they require markets to decline in order to deliver value. However, while tax-loss harvesting can indeed be executed in down markets—where any investment vehicle or investor can realize losses—we believe the real opportunity lies elsewhere. Managers who employ systematic tax-loss harvesting strategies, particularly those holding individual stocks, can add value even in upward-trending markets, provided there is dispersion among those stocks.

The Role of Dispersion in Tax-Loss Harvesting

As portfolio managers, we of course recognize that the best market environments are those where investors make money. If all the stocks in a benchmark were to rise uniformly and never revert, investors might not realize any losses, but would still be pleased with their portfolio's performance. However, markets are far more dynamic in practice: They exhibit volatility and dispersion among individual stocks, even during periods of overall positive returns. This creates opportunities for tax-managed strategies.

To illustrate, let’s examine the performance of the S&P 500 Index in 2024, which was an exceptionally strong year for investors. The market delivered a pretax return of 25%. Below is the growth of a $1 million investment in the S&P 500 over the course of the year.

Growth of $1 Million Investment in S&P 500, Calendar Year 2024

Volatility Within a Strong Market

Given the consistent upward trend in last year’s market - positive from January 17 onward—one might assume that there was limited potential for tax-loss harvesting; however, this is where the importance of holding individual stocks becomes clear. While the overall market rose, we saw meaningful volatility across the individual stocks that comprise the index. The display below highlights the number of stocks that increased in value each month (green) versus those that declined (gray).

Monthly Breakdown of Positive vs. Negative Stocks in the S&P 500 During 2024

Over the course of the year, 172 stocks in the S&P 500 ended in negative territory, with an average loss of 16.6%. Investors who could capitalize on this dispersion had the potential to achieve the market’s full return of 25% while simultaneously harvesting losses. These harvested losses could have then been paired with realized capital gains to provide potential tax savings.

A Broader Perspective

This experience is not unique to 2024. The chart below shows annual S&P 500 market returns over the past 20 years, along with the percentage of stocks that increased (green) versus those that decreased (gray) each year.

Annual S&P 500 Returns With Percentage of Positive vs. Negative Stocks, Since 2004

Harnessing Volatility With a Systematic Approach

As portfolio managers, our ultimate goal is to help our clients grow their wealth through positive market returns. By incorporating active tax management strategies such as systematic tax-loss harvesting, we aim to enhance after-tax returns and deliver greater value to investors.

This material is provided for informational and educational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Investment decisions and the appropriateness of this material should be made based on an investor’s individual objectives and circumstances and in consultation with his or her advisors. Information is obtained from sources deemed reliable, but there is no representation or warranty as to its accuracy, completeness or reliability. All information is current as of the date of this material and is subject to change without notice. The firm, its employees and advisory accounts may hold positions of any companies discussed. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types.

This material may include estimates, outlooks, projections and other “forward-looking statements.” Due to a variety of factors, actual events or market behavior may differ significantly from any views expressed.

Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Investments in hedge funds and private equity are speculative and involve a higher degree of risk than more traditional investments. Investments in hedge funds and private equity are intended for sophisticated investors only. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

This material is being issued on a limited basis through various global subsidiaries and affiliates of Neuberger Berman Group LLC. Please visit www.nb.com/disclosure-global-communications for the specific entities and jurisdictional limitations and restrictions.

The “Neuberger Berman” name and logo are registered service marks of Neuberger Berman Group LLC.

© 2009-2025 Neuberger Berman Group LLC. All rights reserved.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Innoviz Equity Sale Shakes The Narrative (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The CEO of Innoviz Technologies Ltd. (NASDAQ:INVZ), Omer Keilaf, gave no indication on December 23rd to look for an equity sale, only to announce one on February 10th. This shook for many shareholders the authenticity of the narrative relating to other claims made by the company. The excitement around the news releases that afforded a quick 52-week high rise of share prices to $3.14, based on relationship and cooperation with Nvidia (NVDA), and the announcement of the plan for the NRE (non-recurring engineering expense) payments helping to finance the activity of the company for the next three years, have lost their momentum sooner than the actual sale of the equity at 1.39. They completely sunk the mood thereafter.

I was not surprised that Innoviz needed the cash injection. In my recent article commenting on the rapid advance of the share price that lingered for almost six months below the dollar, I declared a sale of equity based on my assessment of the company's financial situation. I understated the larger amount Innoviz spent in the fourth quarter, which left the company with only $67.9M of cash at the end of 2024, as announced during the filing for the equity sale and later confirmed during the quarterly release.

Reducing headcount efforts to save $12M from the operating cash flow consumption of $77M in 2024 would still have left the company with zero cash. Adding $37.7M after fees paid to Westpark Capital for underwriting the direct offering would leave the company with that amount only. Depending on how much of the $40M of NRE would be paid in 2025, in full, partially, or even at all, as the disclaimer indicated in the risk section, that amount would top it.

Innoviz's CFO and CEO discussed a positive gross margin for the entire year and revenue of $50 to $60M, with an inclusion of $40M NRE. However, they did not describe how much of the gross profit would be applied to the cash levels, acknowledging that it would reduce OCF consumption.

Now, OCF spent $68M, and the best-case scenario of $40M translated into additional cash, added to the $37.7M from the equity sale, would leave Innoviz with $78M in cash.

As the table below illustrates, Innoviz still needs a second equity sale to have some cash left entering 2027. Even with $20M of the NRE planned for 2026, there is not enough to operate. I have applied a conservative 10% contribution to cash after all adjustments to forecast that the company will be left with only $46M, highlighting an even greater necessity for an equity sale.

I have also reduced the company's revenue forecast from the ones given by analysts for 2026, while still having doubts about its accuracy. A certain level of supportive bias has not materialized into reality, not just for Innoviz but for all the three Western LiDAR companies' forecasts: MicroVision (MVIS), Luminar (LAZR), and Aeva (AEVA).

Until recently, the forecast was $76.5M for 2025, which the company has now reduced to $60M at the top range. I am forecasting Innoviz to have around $140M in 2026. This is also very optimistic, as there is nothing really to drive this amount with the provision of sensors to Mobileye's (MBLY) Drive platform testing, which adds three prototypes existing just in rendering in some cases. While Innoviz applies nine sensors per unit, I do not see how that amount would generate a revenue of $120M when deducting the $20M NRE expected for that year.

I have left revenue for 2027 and the analyst's expectations, but I doubt that number the most. The nearest example of first-year mass production is Luminar, with its relationship with Volvo and two models to be equipped with Iris. The company is expected to have $116M in revenue, supplying products from already created and operating, accepting not efficiently, supply chain.

Up to this point, Innoviz has made no effort to explain its supply chain, manufacturing, and cost strategies. The company has not named a model for the expected 2027 mass SOP. While the event is two years away and may appear long enough to achieve all of it, in my view, the lack of detail is another question mark on the legitimacy of those statements.

As Luminar can attest, production will be the most draining cash event Innoviz will ever experience. In addition, the revenue predictions are based on non-existent demand today. If a forecast is to be made, this one is purely academic, with zero references to actual business. In short, I do not see how Innoviz can ever achieve $500M in revenue in 2027. Without understanding any of the factors that would lead to a positive gross margin of scaled-up production, an experience that is only learnt when doing it, talking about profitability is clearly unsubstantiated.

The language of integration, cooperation, and partnership with the platform providers and their products does not indicate that they are exclusive, whether it is Mobileye Drive, NVIDIA DRIVE Orin, or any other name tossed into the narrative. While Mobileye named three prototype makers, this does not mean that Mobileye will use Innoviz sensors for every sale of its Drive platform, despite how much the Innoviz team tries to imply it.

The company has not won any new design deals but expects to win one to three in 2025. The business of ADAS applications for consumer cars has been an uneventful daytime drama, with declarations of participation in RFQs and RFIs leading to zero achievement. The market is tired of awarding valuations for participation, as shown in the companies' capitalization, which is at its lowest in years.

In some circles, the emergence of an idea of ADAS without LiDAR, especially for consumer automotive, may also be the kink in the armor along the noncommittal, expensive journey for LiDAR providers held by OEMs in the waiting room. Finally, Chinese ADAS dominance in China through Hesai (HSAI) and RoboSense, with unclear but potentially passable geopolitical barriers, makes OEMs second-guess their future plunge into commitments.

With doubts mounting, another equity offering expected in 2026, and the price of shares below $1, I suspect the company's next move will be a reverse split, an action already approved by the shareholders. Seeing that, I imagine market forces will continue to drive the stock price down, anticipating the unleashing of even stronger selling pressure post-reverse split.

I move my view on Innoviz Technologies Ltd. stock to a strong sell, as no catalysts would reverse the financial condition of balancing greater needs versus limited resources. If I believe Innoviz is a promising startup, I cannot declare a company as a tier 1 experienced entity. If I go with the thesis that Innoviz is a tier 1, it must be the worst of them, as it hasn't sold a single sensor from its production capacity. That identity crisis and lack of predictability with a shaken narrative leaves any expectations with questions, not answers.

While I am sure there will be fluctuations in stock price and perhaps a materialized win with an OEM that will spike the stock up, the execution of those wins, further NREs, and sampling sales have not graduated to revenue from selling the product in what is four years for Innoviz as a public company. In my view, that failure with the broken narrative is not worth the cost for investors that look at LiDAR as a long-term investment.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Axcelis Technologies: Oversold And Undervalued - A Long-Term Growth Play
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Indeed, Axcelis Technologies (NASDAQ:ACLS) is currently facing serious challenges, including the downturn in the electric vehicle industry, overcapacity in China, and growing competition from local manufacturers. However, the company's long-term prospects remain attractive due to several key factors:

  • The growth in demand for silicon carbide (SiC) will continue even with a decrease in EV sales due to the demand for this technology in energy, 5G and industrial electronics. McKinsey expects that by 2027, more than 50 percent of battery-powered electric vehicles (BEVs) will be able to use SiC powertrains, up from about 30 percent today. This shift, combined with the growing preference for BEV among electric vehicle buyers, will increase the demand for SiC.
  • The restoration of the memory segment (DRAM, NAND) by 2026, stimulated by the development of AI and cloud technologies, will create additional opportunities for growth.
  • Semiconductor industry support programs in the United States and Europe (CHIPS Act) will lead to increased demand for equipment, which will mitigate the risks associated with supply restrictions in China.
  • Expansion into the Advanced Logic segment will allow Axcelis to diversify its business and reduce its dependence on the EV market. In addition, CS&I's service area continues to provide stable revenue streams, reducing financial volatility.

Axcelis is also one of the most financially sound companies among its competitors, with high margins, a very low debt burden, and a low share price in terms of P/E and EV/EBITDA multipliers. I believe that the market has oversold the company's shares too much, resulting in a 21% upside to the current price. I am assigning Axcelis a "Buy" rating with a target price of $78 per share.

Introduction

Axcelis Technologies, Inc. is a technology company engaged in the development, manufacture and maintenance of ion implantation equipment used in the manufacture of semiconductor chips. The company holds one of the key positions in its segment, supplying equipment to the world's largest chip manufacturers that use it in electronics, electric vehicles, energy, 5G and other high-tech industries.

The company's main product is the Purion ion implant series, which provide high precision, purity, and efficiency in chip manufacturing. This technology is used to create certain properties of semiconductor materials, which are critically important for their operation. Axcelis also offers after-sales services, including the supply of spare parts, equipment upgrades and after-sales service, which creates an additional stable source of income.

The company's business can be divided into several segments: Mature Process Technology, Memory, Advanced Logic and Aftermarket (CS&I service area). The largest area is Mature Process Technology, which includes chips for power electronics (Power Devices), industrial and consumer electronic devices (General Mature) and image sensors (Image Sensors). Most of the revenue comes from the SiC-based power chips, which are widely used in electric vehicles, energy and industrial automation. Their share in this segment is 51%, and the average annual growth rate has reached 61.7% in the last three years.

The Memory segment, which includes the production of equipment for DRAM and NAND memory chips, has faced instability in recent years. After growth in 2022 and 2023, demand declined sharply, and in the third quarter of 2024, Axcelis was able to sell only one DRAM unit, while sales of NAND equipment stopped completely. This is due to the cyclical nature of the memory market, but it is expected that by 2026, a new growth in demand will begin due to the development of cloud technologies, artificial intelligence and data centers.

The Advanced Logic segment, related to the production of logic chips, still holds a small share in the Axcelis business, but the company is actively working to expand its presence in this area. Logic chips are used in powerful computing systems, servers and processors, and in the future this can become an important source of growth.

CS&I's service line, which includes the sale of spare parts, modernization and maintenance of equipment, consistently brings the company about 20-25% of revenue. This is a high margin business that is less susceptible to market fluctuations, as customers are forced to maintain the functionality of their equipment regardless of market cycles.

Axcelis production facilities are located in the USA and South Korea. Beverly is home to a 417,000-square-foot main plant. In 2023, the company opened a new logistics center in the same city, which increased the efficiency of supply chains. Production processes include modular assembly, which reduces production time, and some components are purchased from third-party suppliers.

The company's main customers are the world's largest chip manufacturers, including TSMC, GlobalFoundries, Infineon, ON Semiconductor, STMicroelectronics, Samsung, SK Hynix and Kioxia. Most of the sales come from international markets, and China plays a particularly important role, accounting for 59% of the total systems shipped in 2024.

The company's long-term development strategy includes expanding the Advanced Logic segment, increasing the share of the CS&I service business, increasing sales of SiC equipment, as well as expanding into new markets, including Japan and Europe.

Competitors

The industry of ion implantation equipment, a key process in semiconductor manufacturing, has been showing strong growth in recent years. According to McKinsey, the market for silicon carbide (SiC), which is a critical material for power electronics in electric vehicles (EV), was estimated at $2 billion in 2023 and is expected to reach $11-14 billion by 2030, corresponding to a CAGR of 26%. This growth is driven by the expansion of the electric vehicle market, which is projected to grow by 20% per year and reach 64 million vehicles sold by 2030, four times more than in 2022. About 70% of the demand for SiC will come from EVs, and China, as the largest consumer of electric vehicles, will provide 40% of the global demand for silicon carbide.

Axcelis is one of the leading players in this market, especially among manufacturers from the USA. In the ion implantation equipment market, Axcelis' main competitor is Applied Materials, Inc., and only Axcelis and Applied Materials offer a full range of ion implantation equipment. Among other manufacturers of ion implantation units, the company competes with Sumitomo Heavy Industries Ion Technology Co. Ltd. and Nissin Ion Equipment Co., Ltd. (Japan), Advanced Ion Beam Technology, Inc. (Taiwan), as well as with CETC Electronics Equipment Group Co., Ltd. (China). About 70% of the demand for SiC will come from EVs, and China, as the largest consumer of electric vehicles, will provide 40% of the global demand for silicon carbide. In the service and spare parts segment, the company faces high competition from local suppliers in the countries where customers operate, who offer alternative service solutions and certified products.

The industry is currently facing a number of challenges and risks. According to McKinsey, in China, 80% of the SiC wafer market and more than 95% of the SiC device market are controlled by foreign manufacturers, but Chinese companies are actively increasing local production. The share of local suppliers in China is projected to grow from 15% in 2022 to 60% by 2030, which creates additional risks for American SiC manufacturers. In addition, the high cost of research and development (R&D) requires significant capital investments, which negatively affects business profitability. The growing competition from Chinese and other Asian manufacturers is putting pressure on prices and forcing companies to increase costs.

Despite these challenges, there are a number of long-term market growth drivers. First, SiC continues to displace traditional silicon (Si) technologies due to its high energy efficiency, better thermal conductivity, and high temperature resistance. McKinsey predicts that more than 50% of electric vehicles will use 800-volt platforms by 2030, further increasing the demand for SiC components. Secondly, government support programs such as the CHIPS Act in the United States encourage local semiconductor manufacturing, which creates additional opportunities for equipment suppliers. Third, demand for DRAM and NAND memory is expected to recover by 2026, which could lead to increased investment in semiconductor manufacturing and increased orders for Axcelis equipment.

Financials

The financial results for 2024 did not meet the expectations of investors, as a result of which the company's shares fell by more than 65%. The company's revenue decreased for the first time in the last 5 years compared to the previous year. By the end of 2024, revenue decreased by 10% compared to the result of 2023 and amounted to $1.018 billion. The main reason for the drop is the slowdown in the growth of the electric vehicle market and the corresponding drop in demand for Axcelis products, "overcapacity" in China and restrictions on exports from the United States.

Most of the total revenue for the year (41%) was generated by SiC, while 31% came from General Mature.

The average annual growth rate of total revenue (OTC:CAGR) between 2018 and 2024 was about 14.89%. I expect that revenue stagnation will continue in 2025 and total revenue will amount to about 1,057 billion rubles, or an increase of 3%. My forecast is based on a scenario in which demand for electric vehicles will remain low in 2025, and Chinese customers will digest the capacity they have already purchased. The management's forecasts are in line with my expectations – they don't expect revenue growth in 2025.

The cost of sales includes the cost of production, SG&A, and 9-10% of all revenue the company directs to R&D. The cost increase in 2024 was mainly due to investments in technology development, staff growth, and high costs for equipment and customer support.

More and more of the company's costs are accounted for by R&D because the company continues to develop its product line and increase the staff of engineers. As a result, I expect the company to increase its R&D costs to 11% of total revenue in 2025 in order to maintain competition in the market.

The company's EBITDA in 2024 fell more than revenue– by 15% to $251 million. As a result, the EBITDA margin also decreased significantly by almost 8% to 18.44%. I expect EBITDA to decrease by the end of 2025 due to lower revenue, but at the same time, I expect that with the recovery of the Chinese market and the electric vehicle market, the company will return the margin level to 20% in 2026. My expectations are supported by the weak growth rate of the company's business, increased staff and R&D costs, as well as the company's market position, which does not allow it to keep margins stable. The average annual EBITDA growth rate from 2018 to 2024 is 25%. According to my forecast, the company will earn about $201 million in EBITDA in 2025, and from 2026 it will return to a growth rate of more than 20%.

Net profit for the year 2024 amounted to $201 million, having decreased by 20% year-on-year. The return on equity (ROE) is about 19.85%. I expect the net profit to continue to decline to $150 million in 2025, but at the same time, with a change in market conditions in 2026, the company's net profit will grow by an average of 13% per year until 2030 due to a low debt burden and small interest payments, revenue growth in the main segments and business development in new markets, in particular in the Japanese market. Thus, we see that despite the difficult period faced by the entire industry, not only Axcelis, the company still remains profitable and one of the most marginal companies among all competitors. I expect that the market cycle will turn in a favorable direction closer to 2026 and Axcelis will regain its operational growth rates and margins.

Axcelis Technologies' financial position remains stable due to its low debt burden and high liquidity. The debt-to-EBITDA (Debt/EBITDA) ratio decreased from 0.73x in 2018 to 0.17x in 2024, while net debt remains negative at -2.10x, which means that the company's cash exceeds its debt obligations. At the same time, the Interest Coverage Ratio reached 43.16x, which indicates an extremely low interest burden and high financial stability. Overall, Axcelis continues to strengthen its financial position by reducing its debt burden and maintaining a high level of liquidity, making it a reliable player in the semiconductor equipment segment.

Thus, despite the difficult market conditions, Axcelis' financial position is stable and the company's growth prospects remain. In the long term, demand for SiC will remain high, especially if the EV market recovers and a new investment cycle in semiconductor manufacturing begins, which will support the company's financial results.

Valuation

After the news came out that the company's management expects a drop in financial results in 2024, the company's share price dropped by more than 65% from historical peaks. Due to the strong oversold stock, Axcelis is becoming one of the most undervalued companies in the industry. To date, the company's shares are trading at a 46% discount to their historical average in terms of P/E and 43% in terms of the EV/EBITDA multiplier. My thesis is that the company is undervalued compared to its competitors, while having strong advantages over them in management efficiency, high profitability, high income diversification, good market share, high average annual business growth rates and no debt burden. Axcelis is undervalued both by peers and by its historical averages.

For comparison, I took Axcelis' key competitors from the ion implantation equipment industry, or companies relatively similar to it from the semiconductor industry. In terms of P/E (TTM) and EV/EBITDA, the company is one of the cheapest among all. At the same time, it is one of the most balanced companies in the industry in terms of profitability, debt burden and growth rates.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 50%), target value of P/E and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 25%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 13.58%.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 17.47x, and EV/EBITDA is 11.6x (I took the average values for competitors).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of Axcelis shares over the year horizon is $78 per share, with a potential increase of 21% to the current price. Thus, I believe the company's shares are undervalued at the moment and I believe that investors have oversold the company's shares, focusing on the short-term problems of the entire industry, ignoring the long-term growth prospects.

Risks

Axcelis Technologies faces a number of serious risks affecting its financial results and growth prospects. The key challenge remains the slowdown in the electric vehicle (EV) market, which has been the main driver of the company's growth in recent years. High interest rates limit the availability of car loans, which reduces demand for EVs. At the same time, automakers are reviewing their strategies: General Motors is reducing plans to produce electric vehicles, Ford is betting on hybrids. Chinese companies (BYD, NIO) are waging price wars by offering more affordable models, which forces competitors to reduce margins. All this affects the demand for silicon carbide (SiC) and IGBT transistors, which Axcelis specializes in.

Another serious risk is the situation in China, where 59% of the company's revenue is concentrated. Local players such as CETC and Kingstone Semiconductor are actively increasing the production of ion implants, creating competition and reducing the market share of Axcelis. In addition, China is undergoing a phase of capacity “digestion” – previously purchased equipment is still in use, which temporarily reduces demand for new installations. Additional pressure is created by US export restrictions, which may limit the supply of equipment from American companies to Chinese customers.

Axcelis is also facing the general cyclical nature of the semiconductor market. Right now, demand for DRAM and NAND memory remains weak, and the company sold virtually no DRAM equipment in the third and fourth quarters of 2024. Advanced Logic has decreased by 73.9%, and key customers are temporarily reducing capital expenditures. This means that the demand for the company's equipment may remain low in 2025.

Despite these challenges, Axcelis has several long-term growth drivers. Firstly, SiC remains in demand not only in the EV industry, but also in energy, 5G and industrial electronics, and the transition from traditional silicon (Si) to SiC will continue in the next 5–7 years. Secondly, the United States and Europe actively support local semiconductor production – CHIPS Act stimulates demand for equipment for new factories of Intel, TSMC and Samsung. Thirdly, the Memory segment is expected to recover by 2026 due to the growing demand for cloud technologies and AI. Fourth, the company is expanding its presence in Advanced Logic, reducing its dependence on EV. Finally, the development of a service sector (CS&I) that generates stable income protects Axcelis from market fluctuations.

Thus, in 2025, the company is likely to face a decline in revenue, but long-term trends point to a recovery in demand in 2026-2027. The key challenge will remain balancing short-term risks with opportunities created by SiC growth, U.S. support, and semiconductor cycle recovery.

Conclusion

I believe that the share price of Axcelis is undervalued, especially after a 68% drop, and I believe that investors are too pessimistic about the company's shares, due to the fact that they focus on short-term results, missing out on long-term business prospects. I recommend buying shares of a company with a 21% growth potential in 2025. Axcelis is currently one of the most financially balanced companies in its sector. The company has significant short-term risks that negatively affect the business now, but Axcelis should be considered as a long-term investment with a 5-10 year horizon.

data.title: WPP plc: Softer Than Expected Q4 Spooking Markets (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
WPP plc (NYSE:WPP) continues to deliver on margin improvements, as we discussed in our previous coverage, but that might be one of the few positives to mention this quarter. Unfortunately, the APAC skew is really working against the company, and the issues in China in particular from a macroeconomic perspective seem to be enduring. Markets have not liked the weaker than expected Q4 which put outcomes at the lower range of the guidance, but even more worrisome is that the revenue guidance for next year is flat to negative despite easier comps. With the IPG (IPG) and Omnicom (OMC) merger, and indeed WPP's own consolidations that it has been doing in spaces like PR as well, which is where multiples are more rich, the competitive landscape could be heating up in the more fair-weather markets. Incremental cost savings are also more limited at this point. Still, with the decline in prices, we think the stock has gotten what is has deserved and is a bit more interesting again as a relative value play in the space where it continues to trade at a peer discount.

Discussing Latest Earnings

The whole sector has been in a bit of a down phase lately, with OMC taking a hit on the IPG merger done on an all-stock basis, and WPP getting hit just now on the latest Q4 results just reported.

The reason for the markets getting spooked is twofold. The first is that the Q4 was simply softer than expected with reported revenue less pass-through costs down 4.2%, versus around a -1% midpoint. The operating profit declined less, thanks to benefits from the ongoing cost savings plan which is around 20% ahead of schedule. While some of the decline was more telegraphed by the loss of a healthcare client already announced in the previous earnings, the other half of the declines can be broadly explained by a 20% or so decline in revenues in China. This is quite severe, and shows that the situation remains protracted in terms of discretionary corporate spending trends in China. Because a lot of the business WPP does is on a project basis, a dry up in discretionary spend can sting results.

The other issue is that while it's bad enough that WPP has hit the lower end of guidance, they are also guiding to a similar 2025 performance as they've managed in 2024 in terms of sales.

The guidance is that they will manage further margin improvements thanks to an incremental 40 million GBP in cost savings. This is part of their ongoing plan in integrating and streamlining previously separate acquired businesses, and that a possible further decline in revenues is going to mean an overall flat operating profit performance. In terms of revenues, this is a disappointing guidance because comps should even be considerably easier in 2025, due to idiosyncratic client losses and the fact that China has been now in a protracted decline for over two years. While further cost improvements are of course welcome to investors, the concern would be if the related stable EBITDA performance might be a little optimistic in that context.

Outside of China performance was more mixed, with some positives and negatives, with ex-Germany Europe doing alright, while Germany and the UK taking a bit more of a hit. North America is also fine. In terms of segments, the most resilient continues to be PR. This is also where the latest FGS disposal made the case that we outlined in our last coverage that that segment implies a pretty compressed multiple on the residual business using that precedent transaction. However, Burson, the residual PR business still at WPP, was not doing as well as the sold FGS business.

Bottom Line

It's not all negative, though. There was a silver lining in that net new business seems to have picked up a bit incrementally, according to management comment. Moreover, there is revenue growth in the top 25 clients, now excluding that lost healthcare client, meaning that at least the remaining accounts are satisfied enough to grow their spend with WPP.

The issues though are clear, which is that the greater skew at WPP plc towards China constitutes a problem that could be protracted for the company. We don't really expect much improvement in China at this point, even towards the end of 2025. Neither does management apparently, even with the benefit of easier comps. In the residual markets, we'd pay heed to the OMC-IPG combination, since that could raise the competitive heat in the US and European markets, which have been more resilient. With even the more limited consolidations of WPP capable of producing 125 million GPB in cost-saving benefits, around 6% of EBITDA run-rates, a combination at greater scale could open up more savings for their competitors in the more favorable markets, and therefore intensify competition.

Nonetheless, with the relative languishing of WPP more apparent after the declines, we continue to look at WPP as the potential value option. Our concern, though, is that limited gas left in the cost savings tank and questionable demand outlook does not support the case for constructive price movements. WPP trades at around 7.2x P/Es given the latest diluted EPS data, compared to around 15x for the broader advertising industry, and against 10.2x and 9.7x for OMC and IPG respectively.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fluent, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Fluent, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Target: Undervalued, But With A Pinch Of Risk
data.description:
In this analysis, I want to focus on why I rate Target Corporation (NYSE:TGT) as a Buy, and explain why I am loading up on their cheap shares. To do this, we will take a quick look at their Q3 '24 Earnings report, assess the situation they are in regarding competition and changing consumer habits, and finally analyze its fundamentals using the 9 Pillar analysis and calculate its intrinsic value using our 4 models.

Introduction

Target Corporation is a company that I hold in my personal portfolio. I bought into it when it was trading at around $120.00 last year, and since then, I have seen some great returns. Though recently, after reporting their Q3 '24 earnings, the stock price fell back to the levels at which I bought it, zeroing out my profits. Yet, I am buying more.

Market Overview

Over the past years, Target has been faced with two big issues: changing consumer habits and increasing competition from giants such as Amazon (AMZN), Costco Wholesale (COST) and Walmart (WMT). The company is divided into two main categories: discretionary and non-discretionary (essential) goods. After COVID-19, a lot of shoppers shifted from physical shopping to online shopping, and as more than 50% of the company's annual sales come from discretionary items (items that can be sold online), TGT lost a portion of its customers to companies such as Amazon, which excel at providing customers with top-notch online shopping.

On the graph above, you can see a sudden post-covid spike in online sales as a percentage of the overall retail sales from 2000 to 2020 in the US alone. In 2020, this percentage was sitting at 14, and since then, that is up to 2025, the worldwide E-commerce sales have increased by another 72.60%, from $4.25B in 2020 to $7.39B. This very well points out the effect changing consumer habits have on TGT, as well as the increasing danger from E-commerce-based competitors.

Furthermore, it is probably quite clear that as the US has had a big recession in 2020 and a few smaller ones years after that, TGT again lost a portion of its discretionary profits as people were willing to spend much less on non-essential, pricier items. Below you can see the recession graph based on the GDP.

Now, of course, recessions have always been, and always will be a factor, so they don't bother me that much. What does bother me, though, is the increase in E-commerce competition. So, to figure out how that has been effecting the company, let's check out their fundamentals, starting with the Q3 earnings report.

Analysis

Q3 '24 Report

Despite some of the company's areas doing quite well, such as the beauty segment, which grew by more than 6.00% YOY, or the digital comparable sales growing by 10.80%, upon releasing their earnings, the company tumbled 22.44%. This was due to them missing their EPS estimates by a whopping 19.57%, with analysts expecting the EPS to be sitting at $2.30, but TGT delivered $1.85 instead, even lower than Q3 '23 EPS of $2.10.

The company's guidance also didn't bring a positive surprise. The company expects the GAAP EPS to range from $1.85 anywhere to $2.45, translating to a full year EPS range of $8.30 to $8.90, which would mean a YOY increase, but despite that, investors seemed not to take the news of low Q4 EPS growth too well.

9 Pillars

Now, as I am a long-term value investor, I always look at the long-term performance of a company, rather than analyzing their short-term headwinds. So with that, let's dive into the 9 Pillar Analysis.

You can see from the image above that the company gets a rating of 6/9, which doesn't look too good, but let's dig in deeper.

First, looking at Pillar 1, the PE ratio, let's see how fairly valued TGT is compared to the net income they bring in. As can be seen, their 5Y PE is sitting at 18.64, which is nice and low, and when we look closer, we can see that currently it is sitting even lower, at only 13.14. So compared to what the company has been trading in the past and compared to the broad market, they are currently quite heavily undervalued.

Furthermore, when we compare its PE to the averaged out PE ratio of its competitors in the Multiples Valuation, Dollar General (DG), Walmart (WMT) and BJ's Wholesale Club (BJ), we will find that sitting at 25.42, showing just by how much cheaper TGT is to its peers.

To carry on, as we are already talking about the Multiples Valuation, let's calculate TGT's intrinsic value. When we multiply its current EPS with the averaged PE of its competitors, we will get an intrinsic value of $239.95, which again shows just how much Target is undervalued compared to the three competitors.

Of course, you might argue that Walmart is growing at a much faster rate than Target... but it is also 3x as expensive, and so in my eyes, Target looks much more attractive.

Financial Growth, Pillars 3, 4, and 5

Over the past 5 years, Target's financials haven't been looking that great. Their FCF has decreased from $7.87B in 2020 to $4.54B in 2024. The net income has fallen from $6.95B in 2021 to $2.78B in 2022, but since then, it has been recovering and in 2024 sat at $4.37B. The revenue, on the other hand, has been rising very steadily at a 5Y CAGR of 2.96%, which is low, but it is better than it decreasing.

What you have to understand is the reason for the net income and FCF decreasing. As we already discussed, the company's market share has been decreasing; of course they have been trying to counter that by innovating, but that hasn't been going so great.

Profitability, Pillar 6

Over the past 5 years, Target's profit margin has decreased from 4.67% in 2020 to 4.06% in 2024, with the ROIC decreasing from 14.92% to 11.50% in the same period. This obviously doesn't show anything great, as with its profitability decreasing, the company is spending more money to make less money, hence generating less net income and FCF.

So overall, with low financial growth, decreasing margins, and danger from competition, Target is looking quite vulnerable. So before calculating its intrinsic value, let's sum up its Bull and Bear cases.

Bull Cases

Target is a very well established company that has had its issues lately, but in my eyes, thanks to the fact that they sell both discretionary and non-discretionary goods, it would be very hard for them to lose an extensively significant portion of their customer base to competition. As I mentioned above, the company has joined the E-commerce race, with its online-based sales rising by 10.80% YOY. This, of course, with the world becoming more and more online-based, is a major push in the right direction.

Furthermore, something I didn't yet mention is that 33.00% of Target's net sales come from private labels, which bring the company more profit than non-private label sales and, thanks to their cheap nature, generate a loyal customer base.

Bear Cases

As we saw through the 9 Pillar analysis, Target's net income and FCF growth aren't looking too well. It is possible that if the company fails to innovate as it mostly did for the past 5 years, it might get run over by competition from E-commerce platforms such as Amazon. This would cost them a large portion of their profits, as most online sales come from discretionary products.

Intrinsic Value

To conclude the analysis, I want to go through the three remaining valuation models: Graham's Formula, Discounted Cash Flow model and the Dividend Discount model.

First moving to the Graham's Formula, I believe that into the next 5 years, YOY, TGT's EPS will grow at the highest rate of 6.00%, medium of 4.50% and low of 4.00%. Averaging these predicted growth rates out, we will get an average growth of 4.83% YOY, in line with the analyst estimates. This will give us an intrinsic value of $121.69 which is 1.89% lower than the current price.

But we still have 2 more models to go through... For the DCF model, I plugged in the YOY FCF growth into the next 10 years + at 4.20%, and with a discount rate of 7.89%, higher than the WACC of the company of 6.79%, I got an intrinsic value of $166.69.

Finally, for the DDM, I plugged in the future dividend growth at 5.00%, lower than the 5Y CAGR of 13.68%, as I do want to be quite conservative, and with the same discount rate as the one used above, I got an intrinsic value of $162.57.

So now that we have gone through the 3 models (I will not use the Multiples Valuation, due to the large amount of differences between TGT's and its peers' products), after averaging them out, we will get an intrinsic value of $150.32 which is 21.18% higher than the current price of $124.04, and so with a margin of safety of 15.00%, we would get an acceptable buy price at $127.77.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I am rating Target Corporation as a Buy. This rating is based on the company's undervaluation, as after going through the 4 models, it is quite clear that even with low future financial and dividend growth, their intrinsic value is higher than their current trading price.

Furthermore, I believe that with the broad market current overvaluation, in the near future, many investors will prefer to invest into more stable, well established companies with lower P/E ratios (cheaper), such as Target. I also trust that the company's well-known name will continue to drive sales, and with growing E-commerce sales, push their stock price higher.

data.title: Installed Building Products: Time To Keep Investors Warm
data.description:
Shares of Installed Building Products (NYSE:IBP) have seen a 40% pullback from highs seen early in 2024, which in itself followed a period in which shares enjoyed great momentum.

The move lower, in combination in with modest growth, which in itself is largely driven by M&A efforts, makes that appeal is rapidly on the increase at a mid-teens earnings multiple. Amidst continued and emerging headwinds, I am looking to buy on smaller dips from here.

An Insulation Play Getting More Diversified

Installed Building Products is one of the nation's largest new residential insulation installers, with 250 locations across nearly all states serving residential and commercial projects.

Over the past decade, the company has become somewhat more diversified with the reliance on insulation falling from 80% to 60% of sales, with adjacent revenues now generated from garage doors, special doors, waterproofing, fireproofing, rain gutters, and alike. Nonetheless, insulation remains the core expertise of the business, with the company claiming a 30% market share in its relevant markets.

The size and diversity of offerings makes that the company can play the role of a one-stop party, operating between the building material producer and the finished home. This one-stop distribution step surpasses the traditional model of distributor, to wholesaler/retailer, to contractor.

The fragmented market and room for decent growth, make that Installed grows with the passage of time, not just organically, but also thanks to regular bolt-on M&A activity.

Amidst the recovery of the housing market post the financial crisis around 2009, and continued focus on ESG, Installed Building has seen a great performance in the past, wholly reflected in the share price.

Over the last decade, the company has grown sales from about $0.5 billion to nearly $3 billion as greater scale allowed the business to grow operating margins from mid-single digits to low-double-digit margins, all while the company even managed to buy back shares. In that light, shares increasing a factor of about 10 times over this period of time is not a surprise at all.

Picking Up The Base

In February of last year, the company reported a modest 4% increase in 2023 sales to $2.78 billion, in part aided by an M&A contribution. End markets were impacted by higher interest rates, putting a dampening on activity.

The company posted GAAP operating earnings of $369 million, equal to operating margins of 13.3%, and this is after a $44 million amortization charge, among others.

Adjusted earnings just topped the $10 per share mark, as the company operated with a net debt load equal to about reported EBITDA. This came after shares saw incredible momentum, as a $125 stock in November 2023 doubled to $250 per share in March 2024, creating a very tough set-up for the remainder of the year.

Ever since, shares have mostly traded in a $200-$250 range for most of 2024, having fallen to the $170 mark in recent weeks.

Modest Growth

Amidst expectations rising significantly early in 2024, the company reported a 5% increase in first quarter sales, as margins came in strong, setting the company up for another year of growth. The company continued its bolt-on dealmaking strategy, as it announced two acquisitions in June of last year, adding a combined $14 million in sales. This was followed by the purchase of Euroview over the summer, a somewhat larger deal which is set to contribute $20 million in annual sales.

These deals supported a near 7% increase in second quarter sales, with the company announcing the purchase of Wholesale Insulation Supply in the fall, adding another $22 million in sales. Operating momentum, aided largely by the M&A efforts, made that third quarter sales grew by nearly 8%, as the acquisition of Tatum Insulation added another $17 million in annual sales.

Given the continued acquisition activity throughout 2024, investors are a bit underwhelmed by fourth quarter sales growth, slowing down to 4%. Full-year sales are up just shy of 6% to $2.94 billion, up some $162 million in absolute dollar terms, to an important extent, but not all, driven by M&A activity with same branch growth reported at 3.5%. The cash flow statement revealed that the dealmaking efforts came at a combined expense of just below $90 million.

GAAP operating earnings were up less than 4% to $382 million, to a minor extent impacted by an asset impairment charge. This and modest net share buybacks made that adjusted earnings were up over 8% to $11.30 per share.

Net debt was reported at $528 million, virtually at par with reported adjusted EBITDA, again with no concern at all.

And Now?

With shares down to the lowest levels since December 2023, while modest advancements were made during 2024, valuation multiples have come in a bit. Based on the adjusted earnings numbers, shares trade at around 15 times earnings, just below the $170 mark.

The company hiked the quarterly dividend by 6%, now payable at $1.48 per share, as this regular dividend is furthermore complemented by a variable dividend of $1.70 per share. Management claims that the long-term outlook for the business remains sound, yet it recognizes that housing affordability remains a challenge for potential buyers. The company furthermore clarified that 9 deals in 2024 added some $100 million in annual sales, and is expected to acquire a similar amount of revenues in 2025.

At current levels, the 28 million shares grant the business a $4.7 billion equity valuation and the business at large a $5.2 billion enterprise valuation. This comes in at 1.7 times sales, around 10 times EBITDA, and represents a 15 times earnings multiple.

Such multiples apply to the wider complex but come amidst hard-pressed organic growth in this environment, solid long-term demand trends, and above-average margins reported by these companies and the industry at large.

Given all this, I am growing more fond of the shares here, but concerns about rates, tariffs and economic growth create a near-term shadow over the shares. Nonetheless, the underperformance means that the shares are gradually getting more appealing, yet for me to start buying into the dip, I need a little better insulation and protection, found at levels around the $150 mark.

data.title: Técnicas Reunidas, S.A. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Técnicas Reunidas, S.A. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.
